phenomenon: folk attribution of moral understanding absent motivation
constructs: moral judgment internalism motivation attribution folk intuitions
claim: people predominantly attribute moral understanding to agents lacking motivation, but this attribution is more lax than moral belief attribution, suggesting partial endorsement of internalist conceptions.

phenomenon: therapist behaviors that establish relational bonds in clinical dyads
constructs: therapeutic alliance relationship quality monitoring goal-setting feedback
claim: therapist behaviors including relationship monitoring, goal clarification, and feedback-seeking are the operational mechanisms through which clinical relationships strengthen and predict session outcomes.

phenomenon: cross-media influence and agenda convergence during campaigns
constructs: intermedia influence advertising agenda news coverage issue selection
claim: political advertising and news media reciprocally shape each other's issue agendas, with both sources influencing the news agenda through distinct causal pathways.

phenomenon: holistic perceptual integration of object parts into unified representations
constructs: holistic representation perceptual integration feature integration complete-over-part
claim: both faces and houses are represented as integrated wholes where recognition benefits from seeing complete objects rather than parts, and this holistic processing is mandatory and probe-delay invariant.

phenomenon: moral evaluation bias in legal attribution of intentionality and culpability
constructs: intentional action blame attribution mens rea moral valence judgment bias
claim: immoral actions bias folk attributions of intentionality toward guilt, which undermines jurors' ability to determine guilt objectively, collapsing legal and moral judgment.

phenomenon: morphological structure effects on parallel word processing in attention
constructs: morphological structure word recognition lexical processing parallel processing flankers
claim: transparent morphological relations between words facilitate processing in parallel attention tasks beyond orthographic overlap, showing that morphological structure modulates attention allocation.

phenomenon: scale range influence on perception of quantity and self-assessment
constructs: response scale category range anchoring comparative judgment self-report
claim: respondents use response category ranges as implicit reference points, inferring population averages from scale bounds and then using those inferences to evaluate their own behavior.

phenomenon: conversational context effects on how questions relate in survey responses
constructs: assimilation contrast question sequencing conversational logic context interpretation
claim: whether part and whole questions are perceived as belonging together determines whether respondents reuse information (assimilation) or interpret the second question as non-redundant (contrast), mediated by conversational norms.

phenomenon: adaptation habituation disruption in experience quality during sustained exposure
constructs: adaptation hedonic decline interruption experience quality enjoyment decay
claim: temporal adaptation diminishes enjoyment of sustained experiences, and strategic interruptions restore hedonic intensity by breaking the habituation process.

phenomenon: economic structural shifts in employment patterns across advanced economies
constructs: deindustrialization employment trade affluence productivity sectoral change
claim: rising consumer affluence, sectoral productivity differentials, and global trade simultaneously drive employment shifts away from manufacturing, with affluence being the dominant factor.

phenomenon: organizational hierarchy effects on wage compensation and responsibility
constructs: firm size wage gaps hierarchy job responsibility managerial span subordinates
claim: wage premiums from firm size increase with job responsibility, reflecting that hierarchical matching amplifies the marginal productivity premium for talent when managing larger subordinate populations.

phenomenon: visual variety effect on perceptual quantity assessment and portion allocation
constructs: perceptual quantity variety Gestalt visual integration quantity estimation portion
claim: variety in a visual array reduces perceived quantity because identical items consolidate into unified Gestalt wholes that appear larger, affecting both judgment and motor behavior like pouring.

phenomenon: expectancy mismatch between source expertise and message certainty in persuasion
constructs: source certainty expertise expectancy violation involvement persuasion incongruity
claim: source persuasiveness depends on expectancy matching: low-expertise sources persuade through certainty while high-expertise sources persuade through uncertainty, by violating audience expectations.

phenomenon: system defense motivation manifested through consumption choice strategies
constructs: system justification system threat consumption defense confidence legitimacy
claim: when systems are threatened, all individuals are equally motivated to defend them, but those less confident use indirect subtle defense while the confident use direct explicit defense.

phenomenon: embodied simulation induced by visual product orientation in advertisement
constructs: embodied mental simulation motor response visual depiction product orientation purchase intent
claim: visual depictions that facilitate embodied simulation of product interaction increase purchase intentions by engaging motor system resources, and this effect reverses for negatively valenced products.

phenomenon: spatial representation of time influencing temporal product perception and attitude
constructs: time cognition spatial location left-right processing fluency temporal products
claim: consumers with left-to-right reading have congruent attitudes toward products when positioned right (future-aligned), mediated by improved processing fluency, with the effect reversing for right-to-left readers.

phenomenon: construal level differences between sellers and buyers affecting price discrepancy
constructs: construal level seller-buyer price discrepancy high-level low-level features desirability feasibility
claim: sellers adopt higher-level construals than buyers, causing them to weight primary features more heavily, so selling exceeds buying price when primary features are superior but secondary inferior.

phenomenon: coping strategies when resources are perceived as limited or insufficient
constructs: resource constraint coping efficiency prioritization goal sacrifice trade-offs
claim: resource scarcity triggers two coping strategies: efficiency (stretching) and prioritization (sacrificing goals), with prioritization increasing under greater constraint despite feeling more costly and often proving insufficient.

phenomenon: cognitive resource depletion from mere presence of salient owned devices
constructs: smartphone presence cognitive capacity attention resources working memory brain drain
claim: the mere presence of one's own smartphone occupies cognitive capacity independent of actual use, reducing available resources for other tasks and impairing performance, especially for dependent users.

phenomenon: age structure effects on entrepreneurial behavior and firm formation rates
constructs: age median demographics entrepreneurship skill acquisition younger older workers positioning
claim: younger societies have higher entrepreneurship rates because older workers in key positions block skill acquisition by younger workers and reduce aggregate entrepreneurial capacity.
